---
title: "Temporal Trends in RSV Wastewater Concentrations Across Vermont NWSS Monitoring Sites, 2023-2026"
date: "May 23, 2026"
author: "Jacob Pancoast"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

pacman::p_load(gratia,
               tidyverse, 
               tsibble,
               slider,
               fable,
               feasts,
               Kendall,
               mgcv,
               knitr,
               kableExtra,
               patchwork,
               cowplot)

opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)

```

``` {r define_constants}

body = 16

font_sizes <- 
  c(
    body = 16,
    h1 = 2 * body,
    h2 = 1.7 * body,
    h3 = 1.4 * body,
    plot_title = 0.8 * body,
    plot_facet = 0.6 * body,
    plot_text = 0.4 * body
  )

```

``` {css, include=FALSE}

h1, h2, h3, h4 {
  font-family: Georgia, serif;
}

h1 {
  font-size: 2em
}

h2 {
  font-size: 1.6em
}

h3 {
  font-size: 1.3em
}

body {
  font-family: 'Open Sans', sans-serif;
  font-size: 16px;
  line-height: 1.6;
}

``` 

<div style ="background-color: #d6d6d6; padding: 10px;">
# Abstract

This is in progress.
</div>

# Introduction

## Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV)

Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a RNA virus belonging to the *Pneumoviridae* family of viruses. RSV infection is most often accompanied by difficulty breathing, coughing, sneezing, congestion, and decreased appetite. Less commonly, RSV can lead to more severe illnesses including [bronchiolitis](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/bronchiolitis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351565) and [pneumonia](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/pneumonia/symptoms-causes/syc-20354204). This type of progression of RSV is more commonly associated with high risk groups, including infants, young children, and older adults^1^.

RSV epidemiology in the United States indicate RSV acivity increase in the fall (respiratory virus season) in the United States, peaking in the winter (December or January) before case numbers lower as spring approaches^1, 2^. In the 2025-2026 season, the CDC identified RSV activity started later in most parts of the US, and also peaked later^3^.

## Why is RSV Significant?

Clinically, RSV presents similarly to the other respiratory infections, including the common cold, influenza, and COVID-19. Due to these similarities, RSV is often misdiagnosed, especially in high risk populations^4^. Worldwide, RSV is a leading cause of acute lower respiratory infections among both children and elderly. In the United States alone, it was estimated to cause between 60,000 and 160,000 hospitalizations as well as 6,000-10,000 deaths annually among adults aged 65 and over in 2023^5^.

``` {r flu_burden, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 1.** Estimated ranges of annual flu and RSV burdens. Figure 1A was obtained from the CDC and shows flu burden from 2010-2025^6^. Figure 1B was created using data from a CDC MMWR report on RSV from 2023 estimating the annual burden."}

flu_rsv_burdens <- 
  plot_spacer() +
  
  (
    ggdraw() +
      draw_image("figures/flu-burden.png") +
      draw_label(label = "Flu Burden", x = 0.5, y = 0.9) +
      draw_label(label = "Figure 1A. Estimated ranges of annual\nflu burden from 2010-2025.\nSource: CDC Estimated Flu Burden⁵", x = 0.5, y = 0.1, size = 10)
    ) +
  
  plot_spacer() +
  
  (
    ggdraw() +
     draw_image("figures/rsv-burden.png") +
      draw_label(label = "RSV Burden", x = 0.5, y = 0.9) +
      draw_label("Figure 1B. Created using estimated ranges of annual\n RSV burden in 2023.\nSource: CDC MMWR on Use of RSV Vaccines; July, 2023⁴", x= 0.5, y = 0.1, size = 10)
    ) +
  
  plot_spacer() +
  
  plot_layout(
    widths = c(0.2, 1, 0.3, 1, 0.2)
  )

flu_rsv_burdens

```

The hospitalization rate for RSV may be between 2 - 4.4 times lower than influenza, while the mortality rate may be between 1.05 - 5.2 times lower than influenza (Figure 1). RSV also exhibits higher rates of pneumonia, hospitalization, and extended hospital stays compared to the flu^7^. Lowering the incidence of RSV would likely improve health outcomes for at-risk populations and reduce costs associated with prolonged hospital stays.

## RSV Monitoring Systems

The RSV-Hospitalization Surveillance Network (RSV-NET) is a CDC-led program that monitors laboratory-confirmed RSV hospitalizations to estimate disease burden and identify trends in virus circulation^8^. Established in 2018, this program serves 14 states (found [here](https://www.cdc.gov/resp-net/dashboard/)) and over 170 counties. The National Respiratory and Eneteric Virus Surveillance System (NREVSS) measures weekly positive RSV tests since 1989 through voluntarily participating labs. RSV is frequently misdiagnosed and testing is underutilized, especially in vulnerable populations (socioeconomically disadvantaged, elderly, children). RSV-NET and NREVSS both suffer from this undercoverage bias in RSV data collection.

Currently, there is no central nationwide surveillance system for RSV in place, leading to large gaps of coverage in certain states.

## Wastewater Surveillance

The CDC collects data reported by state and local health departments through the National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS) supported by funding through the Epidemiology and Laboratory Capacity for the Prevention and Control of Emerging Infectious Diseases Cooperative Agreement^9^.

``` {r NWSS_sites, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 2**. United States NWSS Sites reporting RSV concentrations as of May 14, 2026. Source: CDC Wastewater Monitoring Program^10^.", out.width = "600px"}

include_graphics("figures/nwss_sites.png")

```

Sites across the country (Figure 2) are selected by local governments based on site effectiveness and population coverage. Many health departments prioritize enrolling sites with a high social vulnerability index to better represent vulnerable populations who may be underrepresented in clinical tests for respiratory viruses. 89% of sites are located at a wastewater treatment plant^9^.

Wastewater collection differs between sites. Some sites may sample untreated wastewater (sewage and stormwater) or primary sludge (suspended solids from wastewater). Sampling methods also differ, including grab samples (from a point in time) or composite samples (aggregated grab samples). Sampling frequency also differs between sites^10^. For each sample, various metrics are recorded including flow rate, virus type, viral quantification, and contamination measurements. Each site is also associated with a region and population served^9, 10^. Viral quantification is measured using repeated PCR tests, and all data is submitted to the CDC.

## RSV in Vermont

``` {r NREVSS_dash, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 3**. Percentage of positive RSV tests by state as of the week ending April 25, 2026. Source: CDC NREVSS Dashboard^11^.", out.width = "800px"}

include_graphics("figures/rsv_quintiles.png")

```

As of late April 2026, Vermont had one of the highest proportions of positive RSV tests in the country (Figure 3). This report investigates temporal trends across Vermont's six NWSS monitoring sites from 2023-2026 to assess the burden of the 25-26 RSV season, aiming to evaluate how well the CDC's assessment of the 25-26 season^3^ generalizes to Vermont and discuss Vermont-specific response to RSV.

# Methods

CDC wastewater data for RSV was obtained from Data.CDC.gov. Each row of the data represents a wastewater sample for RSV from participating NWSS sewershed sites, including dates of collection^12^. Before analysis, the data was cleaned and visualized (Figure 4). The "pcr_target_avg_conc" column was chosen for analysis of RSV concentrations rather than "pcr_target_flowpop_lin" since the flow-population-normalized column had large amounts of missing data. Population differences between sites were small enough to accommodate this decision (Table S1). During this step, a seven-day rolling average concentration was calculated for each data point to reduce noise and artifacts. This new column was used for all further analysis. 

All data cleaning, analyses, and visualizations were conducted using R (version 4.5.2; R Foundation).

``` {r clean_data, include=FALSE}
vt_rsv <- 
  read.csv("CDC_RSV_Wastewater_Data.csv") |>
  
  select(site, counties_served, sample_collect_date, pcr_target, pcr_target_avg_conc, lod_sewage, population_served) |>
  
  filter(pcr_target == 'rsv',
         site != '1962') |> 
  
  # Since we filtered for pcr_target == RSV, we can drop the pcr_target column
  select(-pcr_target) |> 
  
  rename(county = counties_served,
         date = sample_collect_date,
         rsv_conc = pcr_target_avg_conc) |> 
  
  mutate(date = ymd(date), # date
         rsv_conc = as.numeric(gsub(",", "", rsv_conc)), # convert to a numeric column and remove commas
         county = as.factor(county), # convert to a factor
         site = as.factor(site), # convert to a factor
         facet_label = paste0("Site ", site, ": ", county, " county"),
         lod_sewage = as.numeric(gsub(",", "", lod_sewage))
         ) |>  # create a title column
  
  arrange(.by = date)

vt_rsv

```

``` {r rolling_avg, include=FALSE}

# Calculate the rolling average column for data visualization

vt_rsv <-
  vt_rsv |> 
  
  select(-lod_sewage) |> 
  
  group_by(site) |> # group by site for calculating rolling average
  
  arrange(date, .by_group = TRUE) |> # arrange by date for calculating rolling average
  
  mutate(mean_7day = slide_index_dbl(
           .x = rsv_conc,
           .i = date,
           .f = ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
           .before = days(6)
         )) |> # create the rolling average column
  
  ungroup() # ungroup the data

```

```{r exploratory_analysis}

exploratory <- 
  ggplot(
    data = vt_rsv
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = date,
      y = rsv_conc
      )#,
    #width = 1
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = date,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = site
    )
  ) +
  theme() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) + labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "RSV Concentration",
    x = "Date",
    color = "Site number",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) + theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["plot_facet"])
  )

```

``` {r graph_colors}

data <- 
  ggplot_build(exploratory)$data[[2]]

colors <- 
  unique(data$colour)

# Define colors
names(colors) <- 
  c(
    1965,
    1966,
    1967,
    1975,
    1976,
    2350
  )

```

### Peak Comparison

Peaks analysis was performed per site to identify per-site and per-season peaks across the data. Each sample was classified based on its respiratory virus season (October through May of the following year) including the start and end date of that season. Season boundaries were defined empirically using wastewater concentration thresholds. Season onset was defined as the first sample date with a concentration greater than the baseline of 0 following an off-season period, while season conclusion was defined as the last sample date with a concentration of 0 following an on-season period. Additional temporal information was added to each row, including days elapsed since season onset. To compare offsets in seasonality, June 1st was arbitrarily picked as an anchor date to classify days since previous off-season. 

From this data, graphs comparing seasonal peaks were constructed. Tables were constructed to compare season lengths, peak days, and peak years for each site.

``` {r seasonal_cols, include=FALSE}

vt_rsv_seasonal <- 
  vt_rsv |> 
  arrange(.by = date) |> 
  
  group_by(site) |> 
  
  # Add season columns
  mutate(
    # Is this date in season onset range?
    onset_criteria = slide_index_lgl(
      .x = rsv_conc,
      .i = date,
      # Onset criteria is defined as dates where all data within last
      # 14 days is above 2 * LOD
      .f = ~ all(.x > (2 * lod)),
      .before = days(14)
      ),
    
    offset_criteria = slide_index_lgl(
      .x = rsv_conc,
      .i = date,
      # Offset criteria is defined as dates where only ~50% of samples during
      # last 14 days had concentrations over 2 * LOD
      .f = ~ mean(.x > (2 * lod), na.rm = TRUE) < 0.5,
      .before = days(14)
    ),
    
    # Add the season column
    season = case_when(
      # If in 2nd half of the year XX, the season is XX - X(X+1)
      month(date) %in% 7:12 ~ paste0(year(date), "_", year(date)+1),
      # If in 1st half of the year XX, the season is X(X-1) - XX
      month(date) %in% 1:6 ~ paste0(year(date)-1, "_", year(date)),
      ),
    
    # Starting year of the season
    starting_year = case_when(
      month(date) %in% 7:12 ~ year(date),
      month(date) %in% 1:6 ~ year(date) - 1,
    )
  ) |> 
  
  ungroup() |> 
  
  # Get rid of off-season
  filter(season != 'off-season') |> 
  
  group_by(site, season) |> 
  
  # Get onset date and conclusion date of each season
  mutate(
    
    # Onset date is defined as the first day in the second half of the year where all samples
    # collected in a 14 day prior period have concentrations above 2 * LOD
    onset = if (any(onset_criteria)) {
      
      # If there is any onset date, we have a minimum to pick as the onset
      min(date[onset_criteria])
      
    } else {
      
      as.Date(NA)
      
    },
    
    # Offset date is defined as the first day after onset where 50% of the concentrations
    # in the previous 14 days were below 2 * LOD
    offset = if (any(offset_criteria & !is.na(onset) & date > onset)) {
      
      # If there is an offset date that exists
      min(date[offset_criteria & date > onset])
      
    } else {
      
      as.Date(NA)
      
    }
    
  ) |> 
  
  mutate(
    
    # Adjust periods outside of season offsets and offsets to off-season
    season = case_when(
      
      # Missing onset
      is.na(onset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Ongoing season
      is.na(offset) & date >= as.Date(onset) ~ season,
      is.na(offset) & date < as.Date(onset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Normal season
      date >= as.Date(onset) & date <= as.Date(offset) ~ season,
      
      # Outside a normal season
      date < as.Date(onset) | date > as.Date(offset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Error catching
      TRUE ~ 'Error'
    )
    
  ) |> 

  mutate(
    
    # Day of the current season
    day_of_season = if_else(
      season != 'Off-Season',
      as.numeric(date - onset),
      as.numeric(NA)
      ),
    
    # DAys since off season
    days_since_june_first = if_else(
      season != 'Off-Season',
      as.numeric(date - make_date(year = starting_year, month = 6, day = 1)),
      as.numeric(NA)
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  ungroup()

```

``` {r plot_seasonal_peaks}

# Graph offset between seasons
seasonal_offset <- 
  ggplot(
    data = vt_rsv_seasonal |> filter(site %in% c(1965, 1966, 1975))
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = days_since_june_first,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = season
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) +
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "Seven-Day Rolling Average of RSV Concentrations",
    x = "Days Since June 1st of Previous Off-Season",
    color = "Season",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = font_sizes["plot_title"],
                              hjust = 0.5),
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["facet_title"])
  )


# Seasonal data by days since start of season
seasonal_peaks <- 
  ggplot(
  data = vt_rsv |> filter(site %in% c(1965, 1966, 1975))
) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = day_of_season,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = season
    )
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) +
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "Seven-Day Rolling Average of RSV Concentration",
    x = "Days Since Start of Season",
    color = "Season",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) + theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["facet_title"])
  )

```

``` {r create_tables, include=FALSE}

# Compare peak days across sites and seasons
peak_days <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  group_by(season, site) |> 

  summarise(
    peak_day = onset[which.max(mean_7day)] + days(day_of_season[which.max(mean_7day)]),
  ) |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |> 
  
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = site,
    values_from = c(peak_day)
  ) 

# Add the average row
peak_days <- 
  peak_days |>
  
  bind_rows(
    peak_days |> 
      summarise(
        across(
          where(is.Date),
          ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
        )
      ) |> 
      
      mutate(site = "average")
  )

  # Compare peak year for each site
peak_year <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |> 
  
  summarise(
    year = season[which.max(mean_7day)],
    .by = c(site)
  )

# Mean season lengths across sites
szn_lengths <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  distinct(site, season, onset, offset) |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |>
  
  mutate(
    
    # Season lengths
    season_length = if_else(
      is.na(offset),
      max(vt_rsv$date) - onset,
      offset - onset
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  select(-onset, -offset) |> 
  
  pivot_wider(names_from = site,
              values_from = season_length,
              id_cols = season) |> 
  
  rowwise() |>

  mutate(
      
    "Average length" = round(mean(c_across(-season), na.rm = TRUE), digits = 2),
    
    "Average length excluding low-data sites" = round(mean(c_across(-c(season, '1976', '2350', '1967')), na.rm = TRUE), digits = 2)
    
  )

```

### Seasonal Decomposition

``` {r seasonal_decomposition_func, include=FALSE}

seasonal_decomposition <- function(site_num) {
  dcmp <- 
    vt_rsv |> 
    filter(site == site_num) |>
    select(date, rsv_conc) |>
    mutate(week = floor_date(date, 'week'), # Change dates to per week
           rsv_conc = log1p(rsv_conc)) |> # Transform rsv_conc by log
    group_by(week) |> 
    summarise(weekly_mean = mean(rsv_conc, na.rm = TRUE)) |> # Create weekly mean
    as_tsibble(index=week) |> # Convert to tsibble
    fill_gaps() |> 
    mutate(
      weekly_mean = replace_na(weekly_mean, 0)
    )
  
  return(dcmp)
}

```

For all further analysis, only the three sites containing consistent data spanning *$n \ge 3$* years (sites 1965, 1966, and 1975) due to inadequate coverage in the remaining sites.

Seasonal decomposition visualizes trends across each site by decomposing a time series ($y_t$) into trend-cycle ($T_t$), seasonal ($S_t$), and remainder ($R_t$) components such that $y_t$ = $T_t$ * $S_t$ * $R_t$. A multiplicative decomposition was chosen due to variation in the trend-cycle. Seasonal decomposition requires continuous, regular time intervals, so dates were grouped by week, log transformed to normalize the right skew, and then averaged for each week. Data gaps were filled with *Concentration = 0* for *n = 3* weeks for site 1966 for the weeks of April 21, 2024; April 28, 2024; and May 5; 2024. After log transformation, the decomposition could be rewritten additively as $y_t$ = $T_t$ + $S_t$ + $R_t$.

``` {r seasonal_decomposition, include=FALSE}

# Get seasonal decomposition for the three compatible sites
site_1965 <- seasonal_decomposition('1965') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

site_1966 <- seasonal_decomposition('1966') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

site_1975 <- seasonal_decomposition('1975') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

```

``` {r get_decomposition_peaks, include=FALSE}

# Extract_decomposition_peaks returns the peak weeks for a site
# from seasonal decomposition

extract_decomposition_peaks <- 
  function(site) {
    
    decomp_peaks <- 
      components(site) |> 
      
      index_by(year = ~ year(.)) |> 
      
      summarise(
        week_max = week[which.max(season_year)], # Get the peak week per season
        max_season_year = max(season_year, na.rm = TRUE) # Get the value of the max peak
        )
    
    return(decomp_peaks)
  }

```

``` {r get_peaks_from_decomposition, include=FALSE}

decomp_peaks_1965 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1965)
  
decomp_peaks_1966 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1966)

decomp_peaks_1975 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1975)

```

``` {r graph_seasonal_decomp, include=FALSE}

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1965
decomposed_1965 <- 
  components(site_1965) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1965"]
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "STL Decomposition of RSV Wastewater Concentration",
    subtitle = "Site 1965: Chittenden County, VT",
    x = "Week",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1966
decomposed_1966 <- 
  components(site_1966) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1966"]
  ) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Site 1966: Chittenden County, VT",
    x = "Week"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1975
decomposed_1975 <- 
  components(site_1975) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1975"]
  ) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Site 1975: Washington County, VT",
    x = "Week"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

``` {r ymd_to_md, include=FALSE}

# Peak_as_month_date takes a decomp_peaks_(site number) and returns the mean peak date
# as a month-date format

peak_as_month_date <- 
  function(peaks) {
  
    max_weeks <- 
      (peaks |> 
         
         filter(
           year != 2023 # Filter out 2023 with truncated data
           ) |> 
         
         mutate(
           week_max = make_date(
             year = case_when(
               month(week_max) < 6 ~ 2024, # Standardize year for the sake of averaging
               month(week_max) > 6 ~ 2023
             ), 
             month = month(week_max), # Keep the month and date
             day = day(week_max)
             )
           )
       )$week_max # Extract only the week_max
    
    mean = mean(max_weeks) # Calculate the mean peak date
    
    return(format(mean, "%m-%d"))
  }
```

### Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

Following descriptive analysis using decomposition, generalized additive models (GAM) were created to model RSV concentration over time. GAMs are useful tools to flexibly model non-linear relationships while maintaining high interpretability by utilizing additive structures. For this time series data, "days elapsed" and "day of the year" were used as predictors for GAMs to model both the long-term and seasonal trends.

Prior to model fitting, building a GAM required modifications to the data. Data was grouped by site, and new columns were created for days elapsed since the site monitoring start date and the day of the year. The seven-day rolling average was also log transformed to normalize the right skew.

GAM models were fit using the mgcv^13^ package for sites 1965, 1966, and 1975. "Days elapsed" and "day of the year" were both fit as a smoothed curve to explain the weekly mean log RSV concentration. "Days elapsed" was based in a thin plate regression spline, which determines knot locations based on the data. "Day of the year" was based in a cyclic cubic spline to account for seasonal trends. REML smoothing parameter estimation was used to reduce the likelihood of oversmoothing the data.

```{r to_gam_format, include=FALSE}

packageVersion("mgcv")

to_gam <- 
  function(site_num) {
    
    data <- 
      vt_rsv |> 
      filter(site == site_num) |> 
      mutate(
        first_date = min(date),
        days_elapsed = as.numeric(date - first_date),
        day_of_year = yday(date),
        weekly_mean_log = log1p(mean_7day)
        )
    
    return(data)
    
  }

```


```{r gam_analysis, include=FALSE}

site_1965_for_gam <- to_gam('1965')
site_1966_for_gam <- to_gam('1966')
site_1975_for_gam <- to_gam('1975')

# Fit gam for site 1965
gam_1965 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1965_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

# Fit gam for site 1966
gam_1966 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1966_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

# Fit gam for site 1975
gam_1975 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1975_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

```

``` {r gam_graphs, include=FALSE}

gam_graphed_1965 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1965, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1965"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1965_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1965, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1965"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GAM Smoothed Components for Site 1965"
  )

gam_graphed_1966 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1966, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1966"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1966_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1966, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1966"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GAM Smoothed Components for Site 1966"
  )

gam_graphed_1975 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1975, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1975"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1975_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1975, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1975"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GAM Smoothed Components for Site 1975"
  )

```

# Results

## Exploratory Analysis

An initial overview of the data sought to visualize early patterns to note trend and seasonal observations, compare sites, and identify any initial limitations of further analysis.

``` {r visualize_exploratory, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 4**. Exploratory data analysis of RSV wastewater concentrations,\n faceted by NWSS collection site. Colored lines represent the calculated seven-day rolling average.\n RSV Concentrations should not be compared between sites^12^; Vermont, 2023-26."}

exploratory

```

Exploratory visualizations revealed clear seasonal patterns around sites consistent with known information surrounding RSV epidemiology. Concentrations begin to increase in the Fall and peak typically in January or February before declining again in April to May. This pattern was consistent across all sites. Only sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 contained three full seasons worth of data, providing enough data for further statistical tests (seasonal decomposition and GAM).

## Peak analysis 

``` {r visualize_seasonal_offset, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.caption = "**Figure 5**. RSV wastewater concentrations in Vermont by day since the start of the on-season; Vermont, 2023-26"}

seasonal_offset

```

Comparison of seasonal peak offsets reveal generally seasons occurring earlier in the study period exhibit earlier onset of RSV concentration increases. The 2023-2024 season rises and peaks early in sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 before the 2024-2025 and 2025-2026 seasons rise. Peaks in the latter seasons appear to be between 30-50 (**QUANTIFY THIS**) days later than peaks in the 2023-2024 season. Data for site 2350 also supports this trend, excluding an early spike in concentration during the 2025-2026 season. Site 1976 maintains fairly similar peaks between the 2024-2025 season and the 2025-2026 season.

``` {r visualize_seasonal_peaks, fig.align = "center"}

seasonal_peaks

```
A total of *n = 4* disjoint respiratory virus seasons were observed among the six sites. Only sites 1965 and 1975 report on all *n = 4* seasons, including truncated data from the 2022-2023 season. Site 1966 excludes data from the 2022-2023 season and reports on the following seasons.

Sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 report high spikes in RSV concentration during mid-late season 2023-2024. Excluding sites 1976 and 2350, RSV concentrations seem to be highest overall in the 2023-2024 and 2025-2026 seasons.

### Season Info {.tabset}

As of May 19, 2026, the `r vt_rsv_seasonal |> distinct(site, season, onset, offset) |> filter(season != "Off-Season", is.na(offset)) |> pull(site) |> paste(collapse = ", "` seasons

#### Season lengths (Table 1)

``` {r season_lengths}

szn_lengths |> 
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 1.</b> Season lengths by site, including the averaged length.") |> 
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

The 2023-2024 season was the longest season on average of the last three years.

#### Season peaks (Table 2)

``` {r peak_day}

peak_days |> 
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 2.</b> Season peak days by site, including the average peak by season and site.") |> 
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

In the 2022-2023, 2023-2024, 2024-2025 and 2025-2026 seasons the most common peak ranges were March, February, February, and late February.

#### Peak years (Table 3)

``` {r peak_year}

peak_year |> 
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 3.</b> Peak year for each site.") |> 
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```


## Seasonal Decomposition

``` {r show_seasonal_decomposition, fig.align = "center"}

decomposed_1965
  
```

For sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 over the last three years, seasonal peaks occur on averange on `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1965)`, `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1966)`, and `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1975)` respectively.

Site 1965 shows a dip in the overall trend of the data during summer 2024. From there the trend continues to increase steadily throughout 2025 and early 2026, where it is currently highest. Site 1966 rises steadily peaks during the 2023-2024 respiratory virus season and has steadily decreased since. Site 1975 also exhibits a dip in the data during the summer of 2024 before peaking again during respiratory virus season 2024-2025 and has steadily declined since then. All sites showed a clear seasonal cycle with a fluctuating long term trend.

Observing seasonal decomposition, we can see after separating the data for site 1965 into the trend, seasonal, and remainder components that the overall trend is increasing for the 2026 season. For site 1966, since early 2024 the overall trend has remained about constant. For site 1975, the trend dramatically decreases during the summer months and is still approaching its peak for the 2025-2026 season.

``` {r show_gam, fig.align = "center"}

summary(gam_1965)
summary(gam_1966)
summary(gam_1975)

gam_graphed_1975

```
The general additive model sums a smoothing spline of days elapsed since March 3, 2023 to a smoothing cyclic cubic spline of the day of the year. For all three sites, data was consistent.

The long-term trend component (days elapsed) shows peaks corresponding to the 2023-2024 and 2024-2025 winter seasons with dips during the summer seasons. The rise towards the right edge of the graph represents the current 2025-2026 season. Towards the edge of the graph the confidence interval widens significantly.

The seasonal component shows a peak in RSV concentrations within the first fifty days of the year, with a sharp decrease that bottoms out around mid-July. Concentrations begin to rise again gradually, before sharply increasing beginning in late October.

For all sets of graphs, confidence intervals tend to widen during the summer months.

# Limitations

A few of the sites used for analysis had gaps in data. Site 1967 only has data for the present 25-26 season, while sites 1976 and 2350 have data for the 25-26 season and the 24-25 season. Site 1976 also has artifacts from the 23-24 season. For this reason, only the three sites with at least three full years of data (1965, 1966, and 1975) were seasonally decomposed and tested using GAM.

A known limitation of the data according to collection methods are that different sampling locations may be collected at different frequencies and different laboratory methods. As such, it was not advised to compare wastewater concentrations across sites. Therefore we will only compare relative trends across sites.

2022-2023 season sucks
some sites don't report all the data
no hospitalizations 
data only covers 4 years maximum which really isn't enough
why was the days elapsed since day chosen
why was june 1st chosen as since off szn
rsv not nationally tested

# Discussion

RSV epidemiology follows a consistent seasonal pattern across the six reporting sites in Vermont from 2023-2026. At a very low p value of <2e-16 [ INSERT CODE HERE SO THIS IS DYNAMIC] (less than machine epsilon) we can reject H0 with overwhelmingly strong evidence at ⍺ = 0.05 and conclude that there is a meaningful non-linear relationship in the data between weekly concentrations and the day of the year.

For the 2025-2026 respiratory virus season, the CDC estimated peak hospitalizations for RSV would be very similar to last year’s, the 2024-2025 season (13). RSV surveillance is limited to wastewater monitoring and positive test results, which focus on infectivity rather than pathogenicity. Hospitalizations better model pathogenicity as ~80% of RSV infections are classified as moderate or mild (14), however infectivity may be a better measure of disease distribution.

As of late April 2026, the NREVSS dashboard suggested Vermont had one of the highest RSV concentrations in the country this late into the season. Two possibilities stuck out as causes for this:

1. Vermont's 25-26 season has exhibited abnormally high RSV concentrations.
2. Vermont's 25-26 season has peaked later than other states this year.

## Viral Distribution Profile

The first aim of this study was to investigate the overall trend for each site and determine if RSV concentrations were statistically significantly higher for the 25-26 season compared to previous seasons. The CDC's respiratory illnesses data channel states RSV burden is no more severe this season compared to previous seasons nationwide (15).

Initially, exploratory analysis corroborated this fact. Most earlier seasons exhibited higher peaks per site than the current season, suggesting this season is well within normalcy. Only sites 1967, 1975, and 2350 peaked in 2025-2026 (Figure 3), yet only site 1975 had enough data to observe a trend. These visual peaks were corroborated by peak analysis (Figure 4,5 ; Table 3). Seasonal decomposition for the three significant sites showed neither sites 1966 nor 1975 exhibited significant increases in the overall trend, suggesting RSV concentrations were within an expected range for this season.

Site 1965's overall trend of RSV concentrations this year increased and reached a new maximum. The trend time portion of the GAM model for this site also suggests this season showed an increase in RSV concentrations, suggesting site 1965 in Chittenden county did show an abnormal increase in RSV wastewater concentrations this year.

## Temporal Shifts

The second aim of this study was to identify possible delayed onset in the current RSV season in Vermont. As of May 15, 2026, the CDC maintains that RSV activity began later than expected in most regions of the US^3^.

Initial exploratory analysis provided no immediate insights into any temporal shift, however peak analysis quickly visualized an offset. For every site except 1967 (due to the aforementioned limitation) and 1976, RSV concentrations started increasing later than previous seasons and peaked later than earlier seasons. Peaks occurred around fifty days later this year among sites 1965, 1966, 1975, and 2350 (Figure 5). Figure 6, which compares peaks across sites and seasons by days since the start of the season, reveals the seasonal trend was not significantly different.

Peak days in the 25-26 season were significantly later than peaks in the 23-24 and 24-25 seasons, excluding site 1976. On average, peaks were around a month later this year (Table 2). Site 2350 exhibited its highest peak on May 5th this year, which shows the extent of this as no site peaked in April or May in the last few years.

Showing the season was offset

- GAM peaks in seasonal corroborate the peaks

# Considerations
- what else could lead to site 1965 showing increased concentrations this year?
- what did vaccinations look like this year? were schedules extended? how did recommendations change?
- this season is lasting longer, so season lengths could still be compared (Table 1)
- why is site 1976 different 
- cite packages

As of late April 2026, Vermont had one of the highest proportions of positive RSV tests in the country (Figure 2). This report investigates temporal trends across Vermont's six NWSS monitoring sites from 2023-2026 to assess the burden of the 25-26 RSV season, aiming to evaluate how well the CDC's assessment of the 25-26 season^3^ generalizes to Vermont and discuss Vermont-specific response to RSV.

did you do this ^^ ?

# Supplemental Figures

``` {r supplemental_table_1}

# Site populations
vt_rsv |> 
  select(site, population_served) |> 
  distinct(site, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  kable(caption = "<b>Table S1.</b> The population served by each participating NWSS site in Vermont.") |>
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

``` {r supplemental_figs}

# Decomposition graphs for sites 1966 and 1975
decomposed_1966 +
  decomposed_1975 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "STL Decomposition of RSV Wastewater Concentration",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System"
  )

gam_graphed_1966
gam_graphed_1975

```


# Sources

1. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Respiratory Syncytial Virus. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/about/index.html. Updated December 30, 2025. Accessed May 14, 2026.

2. Chan EMG, Boehm AB. Respiratory Virus Season Surveillance in the United States Using
Wastewater Metrics, 2023–2024. ACS ES&T Water. 2025;5(2):985-992.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/acsestwater.4c01013.

3. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Respiratory Illnesses. https://www.cdc.gov/respiratory-viruses/data/. Updated May 20, 2026. Accessed May 21, 2026.

4. Davido B, Loubet P. The silent surge: The under-recognised burden of respiratory syncytial virus, human metapneumovirus, and Parainfluenza viruses in adults. International Journal of Infectious Diseases. 2025;159:108006. DOI: http://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijid.2025.108006.

5. Melgar M, Britton A, Roper LE, et al. Use of Respiratory Syncytial Virus Vaccines in Older Adults: Recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2023;72:793–801. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.mm7229a4.

6. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Flu Burden. https://www.cdc.gov/flu-burden/php/about/index.html. Updated February 25, 2026. Accessed May 13, 2026.

7. Recto CG, Fourati S, Khellaf M, et al. Respiratory Syncytial Virus vs Influenza Virus Infection: Mortality and Morbidity Comparison Over 7 Epidemic Seasons in an Elderly Population. J Infect Dis. 2024;230(5):1130-1138. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jiae171

8. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/php/surveillance/rsv-net.html

9. Adams C, Bias M, Welsh RM, et al. The National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS): From inception to widespread coverage, 2020-2022, United States. Sci Total Environ. 2024;924:171566. DOI: http://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.171566.

10. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC's Wastewater Monitoring Program. https://www.cdc.gov/wastewater/about/index.html?cove-tab=3. Updated May 13, 2026. Accessed May 14, 2026.

11. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. The National Respiraory and Enteric Virus Surveillance System (NREVSS). https://www.cdc.gov/nrevss/php/dashboard/index.html. Updated June 18, 2025. Accessed May 14, 2026.

12. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC Wastewater Data for RSV. https://data.cdc.gov/Public-Health-Surveillance/CDC-Wastewater-Data-for-RSV/45cq-cw4i/about_data. Updated May 18, 2026. Accessed May 19, 2026.

13. Wood SN. Fast stable restricted maximum likelihood and marginal likelihood estimation of semiparametric generalized linear models. J R Stat Soc Series B Stat Methodol. 2011;73(1):3-36. doi:10.1111/j.1467-9868.2010.00749.x






https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/respiratory-syncytial-virus-(rsv)


https://otexts.com/fpp3/components.html

10. 

11. https://www.cdc.gov/wastewater/about/index.html

12. 

13. https://www.cdc.gov/cfa-qualitative-assessments/php/data-research/season-outlook25-26.html

14. Belongia EA, King JP, Kieke BA, et al. Clinical Features, Severity, and Incidence of RSV Illness During 12 Consecutive Seasons in a Community Cohort of Adults ≥60 Years Old. Open Forum Infect Dis. 2018;5(12):ofy316. Published 2018 Nov 27. doi:10.1093/ofid/ofy316

15.
